title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Trading Volumes and Spot Prices Rise with a Decline in Average Type 1 Prices
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(24th March â€“ 30th March)
  • A total of 395,313 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 95.50% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades also witnessed a 1.82x times rise WoW as 1,774,652 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price fell this week to $58.76/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 1.97%Â decline WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 0.05% in price, reaching $60.94/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 6.36% WoW; on 02nd April, the spot price stood at $56.25/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $59.88/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices remained stable WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 6.85% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, CA LCFS trading activity surged, with a sharp increase in Type 1 trade volumes and overall credit exchanges. Despite this rise in trading, average Type 1 credit prices saw a slight decline, while broader non-zero transactions prices remained relatively stable. The spot credit market also experienced a modest dip in prices. Meanwhile, RIN prices were mixed, with D3 RINs holding steady while other categories witnessed noticeable gains. On the regulatory front, uncertainty continues around the LCFS amendments following their disapproval, but CARB is actively working on revisions for a swift implementation, aiming for early 2025, though delays could push it further into 2026 as is indicated by CARB at NACW Conference.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_594735" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.97% WoW fall, decreasing from $59.94 to $58.76 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 0.05% WoW, from $60.97 to $60.94. Type 1 trades saw 395,313 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 95.50% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a 1.82x times rise to reach 1,774,652 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_594736" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 1.17% WoW, reaching $59.05/MT. On 2nd April, the average spot price was $56.25/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $59.88/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_594737" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 96 trades were reported in the market, with 19 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 10,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $47.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 5,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 2,122 credits of the Type 3 category, undertaken for a price of $59.00. The largest volume transfers of 150,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for prices of $55.25 and $71.78.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_594738" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] As of March 31st, the average price of Spot Type 1 declined to $58.40 with a total volume of 5.02M, marking a 19.06% drop in price and a 20.44% decline in volume compared to February, when prices were at $72.15 and volumes reached 6.31M.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_594739" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 24th March â€“ 30th March, total VPT recorded a 0.38% decrease (WoW), with a decline in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices remained stable WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 6.85% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Washington State House and Senate Put Forth Bills to Hike Tax Rates on Fuel Licensees. Under Washington State's Biennial Transportation Budget Proposals, the Senate has proposed a 6-cent increase in the tax rate on fuel licensees, while the House has proposed a 9-cent increase per gallon of fuel. These hikes are proposed to take effect on July 1, 2025. Both Chambers have proposed that the tax rate be adjusted annually for inflation from July 1, 2026. Beginning July 1, 2025, the House bill has also proposed an additional and cumulative tax rate of 3 cents per gallon of special fuel on fuel licensees.
  • EPA v. Calumet Shreveport Refining Case on SRE Challenges Heard at Supreme Court. During the Supreme Court hearing on EPA v. Calumet Shreveport Refining, L.L.C. on March 25, 2025, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) defended its mass denial of Small Refinery Exemptions. Citing a new law interpretation and introducing the "passthrough theory," the EPA argued that refineries could pass compliance costs to customers. To avoid inconsistent rulings across circuits and conserve judicial resources, the EPA sought centralized review in the D.C. Circuit Court.
  • Airbus Accelerates Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Adoption with Book and Claim. Airbus is taking a significant step toward scaling the adoption of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) by testing a new â€œBook and Claimâ€ approach. The first MoU was signed by SMBC Aviation Capital, followed by AerCap, Comlux, Luxaviation, Novespace, Rive Private Investment and SAF Aerogroup, who confirmed their interest in joining this collaborative initiative. In simple terms, the book and claim approach allows a buyer to "book" a certain amount of SAF and "claim" the corresponding emission reduction, even if the fuel is used elsewhere. Through a pilot programme running throughout 2025, Airbus will leverage this system to improve SAF accessibility for potential customers, particularly those with limited volumes and far from supply points.
  • HF 943 Prohibiting Eminent Domain on Agricultural Land for CO2 Pipeline Projects Passes Iowa House. HF 943 was passed in the Iowa House of Representatives with 82 yeas and 12 nays. The bill introduced at HSB 287 prohibits the use of the eminent domain argument on agricultural land for CO2 pipeline projects which it defines as "hazardous liquid pipelines for the transportation or transmission of liquefied carbon dioxide". SF 92 was introduced in the Iowa Senate with the same goal and is pending a scheduled hearing.
  • Trump Administration Tells Oil and Biofuels Groups to Hash Out New Biofuel Policy.S. President Donald Trump's administration has asked oil and biofuels producers to hash out a deal on the next phase of the nation's biofuels policy to avoid the kind of political clashes that marked his first term, according to four people familiar with the matter. The White House directive has already yielded at least two bilateral meetings, including one hosted last week by the American Petroleum Institute, said the sources, who include Will Hupman, API's vice president of downstream policy, and three others who asked not to be named.
  • KAG Canada Deploys IFSâ€™ Hydrogen-Fueled Class 8 Heavy-Duty Truck in Commercial Operations. KAG Canada and Innovative Fuel Systems (IFS) announced that KAG Canadaâ€™s commercial operation is successfully using a hydrogen dual-fuel-powered heavy-duty (HD) truck powered by IFSâ€™ patented Multi-Fuel Technology Platform (MFTPâ„¢). MFTPâ„¢ is a retrofit system that can be used on existing HD trucks, allowing a diesel engine to offset diesel fuel usage with cleaner burning fuels like natural gas (in the form of CNG) or renewable natural gas (RNG), and IFSâ€™ new MFTPâ„¢ model can incorporate hydrogen fuel, providing HD fleets with an additional cleaner burning fuel option.
